text: This article examines manifest inter-party conflict over policy issues and the capacity of coalition agreements to resolve these disputes. Drawing on empirical evidence from Germany, Belgium, Italy, and the Netherlands, we analyze the characteristics of these agreements—including specific deals struck over controversial topics—and track the conflicts that emerged during the lifetimes of eleven different governments. Specifically, we investigate how often parties in power are constrained by written records of past negotiations. Our findings reveal that coalition agreements in all four countries contain substantial policy mandates, with roughly one-third of these deals being precisely defined. These agreements do not merely reflect easy consensus; they frequently tackle highly contentious issues. Our central finding is that when intra-coalition conflict occurs, parties almost invariably return to the original policy deals if they were documented at the start of the term. Consequently, rather than being mere "cheap talk," coalition agreements serve as vital blueprints that facilitate collective decision-making and stabilize governance across diverse political systems.
